bio.bio_blurb: Harvard mathematician; first master of Lowell House

notable.notable_blurb: Harvard geometer; first master of Lowell House

narrative_blocks.header: He taught a future president at Groton
narrative_blocks.body: Before joining Harvard, Coolidge taught at Groton, where one of his pupils was Franklin Delano Roosevelt; the two remained friends for life. He went on to spend his whole career at Harvard, chairing its mathematics department.

narrative_blocks.header: He held Oxford’s first science degree
narrative_blocks.body: After Harvard, Coolidge took the very first Bachelor of Science ever awarded by Oxford, in 1897, then a doctorate at Bonn under Eduard Study. His treatises on the circle, the sphere, and non-Euclidean geometry became standards.

narrative_blocks.header: He built Lowell House from raw brick
narrative_blocks.body: Named the first master of Lowell House in 1930, Coolidge had to invent the residential culture of a building “new and raw from the builders’ hands.” His vision shaped Harvard’s house system for generations.

narrative_blocks.header: A geometer who commanded two thousand men
narrative_blocks.body: During the First World War, Coolidge served as a major and liaison officer to the French General Staff, with two thousand American men under his command in Paris. France made him a chevalier of the Legion of Honor in 1919.

narrative_blocks.header: In retirement he wrote mathematics’ history
narrative_blocks.body: After retiring in 1940, Coolidge turned to the history of his field, publishing studies of geometrical methods and "The Mathematics of Great Amateurs." He was a record-setting miler in his Oxford days and a lifelong oarsman.

cross_connections.display_label: a fellow eminent American mathematics professor of the era

bio.bio_blurb: Linguist; matriarch of a Harvard academic dynasty

narrative_blocks.header: Her marriage seeded a Harvard dynasty
narrative_blocks.body: A linguist by training, Theresa Reynolds married the mathematician Julian Lowell Coolidge in 1901 and bore seven children. Among them were a Fogg Museum director and a daughter who married Boston’s leading cultural historian.

bio.title: Dr.
bio.bio_blurb: Harvard professor of obstetrics for over twenty years

notable.notable_blurb: Harvard professor of obstetrics; physician in Boston

narrative_blocks.header: A classicist pressed into the family trade
narrative_blocks.body: Reynolds excelled in the classics at Boston Latin and taught there two years before his father pressed him into Harvard Medical School. He took to obstetrics, and held the Harvard chair in the subject for more than two decades until 1885.

narrative_blocks.header: He married a granddaughter of Paul Revere
narrative_blocks.body: In 1859 Reynolds married Jane Minot Revere, granddaughter of the Revolutionary silversmith. The match folded the Revere line into a family already two generations deep in Boston medicine, with his father the founding surgeon of the eye-and-ear infirmary.

narrative_blocks.header: His own grandson couldn’t name his colleagues
narrative_blocks.body: A book of his medical correspondence passed to a grandson who, in 1913, wrote the hospital librarian that he could not vouch for the handwritten list of names inside or recognize the hand. The doctor’s working world had grown unreadable within two generations.

cross_connections.display_label: his father-in-law, who ran the Revere copper works

bio.bio_blurb: Granddaughter of Paul Revere; Boston matriarch

narrative_blocks.header: Through her, the Revere name entered Boston medicine
narrative_blocks.body: Jane Minot Revere was a granddaughter of Paul Revere and daughter of the copper magnate Joseph Warren Revere. Her 1859 marriage to Dr. John Phillips Reynolds braided the Revere line into three generations of Boston physicians.

narrative_blocks.header: Her son bought back the Revere house
narrative_blocks.body: Among Jane’s eight children was John Phillips Reynolds Jr., who in 1902 purchased and preserved her grandfather’s house in Boston’s North End. The Revere ancestry she carried became the cause that defined his life.

narrative_blocks.header: Her name carried two Boston dynasties
narrative_blocks.body: Jane Minot Revere bore the names of two old Boston families, the Minots and the Reveres, at the moment they joined the medical Reynoldses. Her children’s names, Paul Revere Reynolds and Mary Robbins Reynolds among them, kept the ancestry deliberately visible.

burialCemetery.name: Cambridge Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Cambridge Middlesex County MA. Burial site of Dr. Anson Hooker (H01414) and descendants. FindAGrave Cemetery #90841.
